content:
First, rephrase the problem in graph‐theoretic terms.  Think of the 1000×1000 grid as a graph whose vertices are the 1 000 000 cells, with edges joining cells that share a side.  A domino is then an edge of this graph (it covers the two cells at its ends), and the condition “no two dominoes are adjacent” says exactly that no two chosen edges may be joined by any other edge of the grid.  In graph theory language we seek a largest *induced matching* in the 1000×1000 grid.

1.  A quick density argument via 2×2 sub‐squares shows that you cannot exceed one domino per 4 cells.  Indeed, in any 2×2 block of 4 cells there is room for at most one domino without creating a side‐adjacency to another domino.  Hence the density of dominoes is at most 1/4, so in a 1 000 000‐cell grid you can never go above 250 000 dominoes.

2.  On the other hand one can attain exactly density 1/4 by tiling the board with 4×4 blocks and in each 4×4 block placing exactly 4 dominoes in an “X–box” pattern, for example

   • Horizontal dominoes at (1,2)–(1,3) and at (4,2)–(4,3),  
   • Vertical dominoes   at (2,1)–(3,1) and at (2,4)–(3,4),

   (where the block’s rows are numbered 1…4, its columns 1…4).  One checks that these four dominoes do not touch one another along an edge, and when you tile the entire 1000×1000 board by copies of this 4×4 pattern (with suitable shifts), no two dominoes ever become edge‐adjacent across block‐boundaries.

Since we have both
   an upper bound of (1000 × 1000)/4 = 250 000 and
   a concrete construction achieving 250 000,

the maximum number of non‐adjacent dominoes is 

\boxed{250000}.
